nodes.text: The Effects of Cognitive Load on Decision Making

runs.text: The Effects of Cognitive Load on Decision Making

nodes.text: Jane Doe

runs.text: Jane Doe

nodes.text: University of Example

runs.text: University of Example

nodes.text: Abstract

runs.text: Abstract

nodes.text: This study examined the relationship between cognitive load and decision-making accuracy. Participants (N = 150) completed a series of decisions under varying cognitive load conditions. Results indicate that higher cognitive load significantly reduced decision accuracy, F(2, 147) = 12.34, p < .001. These findings have implications for understanding decision-making in high-stakes environments.

runs.text: This study examined the relationship between cognitive load and decision-making accuracy. Participants (N = 150) completed a series of decisions under varying cognitive load conditions. Results indicate that higher cognitive load significantly reduced decision accuracy, F(2, 147) = 12.34, p < .001. These findings have implications for understanding decision-making in high-stakes environments.

nodes.text: Introduction

runs.text: Introduction

nodes.text: Decision-making is a fundamental cognitive process that has been extensively studied in psychology and neuroscience. However, the impact of cognitive load on decision quality remains poorly understood (Smith, 2020; Johnson & Lee, 2019). The present study aims to address this gap in the literature.

runs.text: Decision-making is a fundamental cognitive process that has been extensively studied in psychology and neuroscience. However, the impact of cognitive load on decision quality remains poorly understood (Smith, 2020; Johnson & Lee, 2019). The present study aims to address this gap in the literature.

nodes.text: Cognitive load theory suggests that working memory has limited capacity (Sweller, 1988). When this capacity is exceeded, decision quality may suffer. Previous research has demonstrated this effect in medical diagnosis (Patel et al., 2018), financial decision-making (Zhang, 2021), and consumer choice (Brown, 2020).

runs.text: Cognitive load theory suggests that working memory has limited capacity (Sweller, 1988). When this capacity is exceeded, decision quality may suffer. Previous research has demonstrated this effect in medical diagnosis (Patel et al., 2018), financial decision-making (Zhang, 2021), and consumer choice (Brown, 2020).

nodes.text: Method

runs.text: Method

nodes.text: Participants

runs.text: Participants

nodes.text: A total of 150 undergraduate students (82 female, 68 male; M age = 19.8 years, SD = 1.5) participated in this study for course credit.

runs.text: A total of 150 undergraduate students (82 female, 68 male; M age = 19.8 years, SD = 1.5) participated in this study for course credit.

nodes.text: Materials and Procedure

runs.text: Materials and Procedure

nodes.text: Participants completed a computerized decision-making task while simultaneously performing a secondary task to manipulate cognitive load. The secondary task involved tracking a moving stimulus on screen (low load) or counting backward by threes (high load).

runs.text: Participants completed a computerized decision-making task while simultaneously performing a secondary task to manipulate cognitive load. The secondary task involved tracking a moving stimulus on screen (low load) or counting backward by threes (high load).

nodes.text: Results

runs.text: Results

nodes.text: A one-way ANOVA revealed a significant main effect of cognitive load on decision accuracy, F(2, 147) = 12.34, p < .001, η² = .14. Post-hoc comparisons using Tukey's HSD indicated that accuracy was significantly lower in the high load condition (M = 62.3%) compared to both the low load (M = 78.9%) and control (M = 81.2%) conditions.

runs.text: A one-way ANOVA revealed a significant main effect of cognitive load on decision accuracy, F(2, 147) = 12.34, p < .001, η² = .14. Post-hoc comparisons using Tukey's HSD indicated that accuracy was significantly lower in the high load condition (M = 62.3%) compared to both the low load (M = 78.9%) and control (M = 81.2%) conditions.

nodes.text: Discussion

runs.text: Discussion

nodes.text: The results support the hypothesis that cognitive load impairs decision-making accuracy. This finding is consistent with previous research and extends our understanding of the mechanisms underlying decision fatigue.

runs.text: The results support the hypothesis that cognitive load impairs decision-making accuracy. This finding is consistent with previous research and extends our understanding of the mechanisms underlying decision fatigue.
